results.possibleIdentity: Kent Holloway was an artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s, notably associated with the early development of the Exploratorium in San Francisco and the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Along with fellow artist Bob Miller, Holloway developed one of the museum's inaugural exhibits. Their work involved 'painting with light,' described as using broad brush strokes of color from the sun to paint the air, rather than traditional pigments. This work was initially developed in a friend's basement and became a foundational piece of the Exploratorium's collection. Holloway was part of a group of artists and engineers in San Francisco interested in the E.A.T. initiative, which fostered collaboration between the arts and sciences. In 1971, he appeared in the film 'Collaborative Drawing,' which documented him and Tom Ryan creating a massive eighteen-by-six-foot pastel drawing. Earlier in his career, during the late 1950s and early 1960s, he was associated with the San Francisco Renaissance and Beat scene, where he was known for symbolist painting and worked alongside figures such as poet ruth weiss and Philip Whalen. He is often identified as Kent Holloway (1935–2004), whose oeuvre includes portraits, nudes, and landscape works like 'Coney Island' (1966).
